walkthrough.title: Unfold the Game Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get Rajas of the Ganges set up. Start by unfolding the main game board and placing it in the center of the table. This board is where all the action happens. You will see four distinct worker placement areas around the edges: the Quarry at the top, the Market on the right, the Palace at the bottom, and the Harbor with the river running along the left. In the middle you will notice two tracks running around the perimeter of the board — the fame track with stars and the money track with coins. These two tracks run in opposite directions, and that is the key to winning.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Fame and Money Tracks
walkthrough.text: Look at the two tracks running around the board's edge. The fame track runs clockwise, starting from zero. The money track runs counter-clockwise, also starting from its own zero position. Each player will place one marker on each track at the starting position. When your fame marker and money marker meet or pass each other, you win. This is the core concept of the entire game — you are racing from both directions to close the gap.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Province Tiles
walkthrough.text: Find all the province tiles. These are the square tiles showing buildings and markets with road connections on their edges. Sort them by their backs — they come in different categories. Shuffle each stack separately and place them face up in the designated supply areas near the Quarry section of the board. Some tiles show buildings that award fame points, and others show market stalls that generate money. You will be buying these tiles and adding them to your personal province board throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the River
walkthrough.text: Look at the Harbor area on the left side of the board. You will see the river — a winding path of spaces leading from a starting dock down through various bonus spaces. Place the river bonus tokens on their marked spaces if they are not already printed on the board. The river is one of the most versatile areas in the game, offering dice, karma, money, fame, upgrades, and market scorings as you sail your boat downstream.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Dice Supply
walkthrough.text: Dice are the most important resource in Rajas of the Ganges. Find all the dice in four colors: yellow, orange, green, and brown. Place them in a shared supply near the board, sorted by color. Each color corresponds to different uses and palace chambers. When you gain a die during the game, you always roll it immediately and place it on your Kali statue. Remember this — you do not choose the value, you roll it right away.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Player Components
walkthrough.text: Each player takes a player board — this is your personal province where you will place tiles. It shows your residence in the center with roads extending outward. Each player also takes a Kali statue. This is the plastic piece with arms that hold your dice. It can hold up to ten dice at a time. Take your two track markers in your color and place one on the fame track start and one on the money track start. Finally, take three workers in your color — these are the pieces you will place on the main board to take actions.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Starting Dice
walkthrough.text: Each player receives starting dice based on turn order. The first player gets one die, the second player gets two dice, and so on. When you receive your starting dice, roll each one immediately and place it on your Kali statue. The colors you receive are specified in the rules based on player count. These dice are your starting currency for buying tiles, using the palace, and sailing the river, so guard them carefully.

walkthrough.title: Set the Karma Track and Choose First Player
walkthrough.text: Each player starts at karma level one on the karma track on their player board. Karma lets you flip dice to their opposite face — one through six, two through five, three through four. This is incredibly powerful for hitting exact values you need. Now choose a starting player randomly. Give them the first player marker. Play proceeds clockwise. You are ready to begin your rise as a raja or rani of the Ganges.

summary.title: Set the Karma Track and Choose First Player

walkthrough.title: The Goal — Two Tracks, One Race
walkthrough.text: The entire game revolves around one goal: make your fame marker and money marker meet or pass each other on the board. Fame advances clockwise and money advances counter-clockwise. The first player whose markers meet or cross wins the game immediately. This means every single action you take should be pushing one of those two markers closer to the other. Some players focus on fame, some on money, and the best players push both simultaneously.

walkthrough.title: Turn Structure — Place a Worker
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you do one thing: place one of your workers on an available action space on the main board. Most spaces can only hold one worker, so if someone already took a space, it is blocked for the rest of the round. After placing your worker, immediately resolve the action of that space. If you have no workers left to place, you must pass. Once all players have passed, the round ends, everyone retrieves their workers, and a new round begins. There is no set number of rounds — the game ends the moment someone's markers meet.

walkthrough.title: Dice — Your Most Precious Resource
walkthrough.text: Dice are the currency that powers almost everything in Rajas of the Ganges. You gain dice from various actions and river spaces. When you gain a die, you always roll it immediately and place it on your Kali statue. Your Kali can hold a maximum of ten dice. If you would gain an eleventh, you must decline it. You spend dice by removing them from your Kali and returning them to the supply. The color and the value both matter — some actions require specific colors, and some require specific values. This is why karma is so powerful — it lets you change a die's value to exactly what you need.

walkthrough.title: Area I — The Quarry (Building)
walkthrough.text: The Quarry is where you buy province tiles to build your personal province. When you place a worker here, you pay the money cost listed on the space, then spend colored dice matching the tile you want to purchase. Each tile shows which color dice it requires. Once purchased, you must immediately place the tile on your province board. Tiles must connect via roads to your central residence or to other tiles you have already placed. Building tiles give you fame, and market tiles give you market stalls that can be scored for money later. There are four building types that can be upgraded for additional rewards.

walkthrough.title: Placing Province Tiles
walkthrough.text: When you place a tile on your province board, the roads on the tile's edges must connect logically to your existing road network. Your residence sits in the center, and roads extend outward from it. Tiles can show buildings, markets, or both. Building tiles typically show one of four types — each type can be upgraded once for a bonus effect. When you connect a long road from your residence all the way to the edge of your province, you often earn a bonus. Planning your tile placement is crucial because a poorly placed tile can block future expansion.

walkthrough.title: Area II — The Market
walkthrough.text: The Market is where you score money from the market stalls on your province tiles. There are two types of market scoring. The first type is assorted goods — you score money for having one market stall of each different kind in your province. The more variety you have, the more you earn. The second type is goods of one kind — you spend a die and score money for all market stalls of a single type, up to the value shown on the die. So a die showing five lets you count up to five stalls of one type. Both scoring methods advance your money marker on the counter-clockwise track.

walkthrough.title: Area III — The Palace
walkthrough.text: The Palace is the most varied area, with multiple chambers offering different effects. The outer terrace is free — place a worker here to gain two money and reroll one of your dice. The terrace lets you gain one die of a specific color for free. The balconies let you trade one die for two dice of another color. And the inner chambers are numbered one through six — you must spend a die showing that exact value to use the corresponding chamber. Each chamber offers a unique powerful effect.

walkthrough.title: Palace Chambers (1-6)
walkthrough.text: The six palace chambers each require spending a die of the matching value. Chamber one is the Great Mogul — it grants a substantial fame or money bonus. Chamber two is the Dancer — she lets you advance on both tracks. Chamber three is the Yogi — he grants karma and possibly dice. Chamber four is the Raja — granting province-building advantages. Chamber five is the Master Builder — offering upgrades to your buildings. Chamber six is the Portuguese — providing trading opportunities and resources. The key challenge is having a die showing the exact number you need, which is where karma shines.

walkthrough.title: Area IV — The Harbor (River)
walkthrough.text: The Harbor lets you advance your boat along the river. To use it, place a worker on a Harbor space and spend a die showing a value of one, two, or three. Your boat advances that many spaces along the river. Each river space you land on or pass through gives you a bonus — these can be extra dice, karma points, money, fame, building upgrades, or even market scorings. The river is one of the best sources of free resources in the game. Some river spaces are especially powerful, offering multiple bonuses or rare effects like gaining an extra worker.

walkthrough.title: Karma — Flipping Your Fortune
walkthrough.text: Karma is tracked on your player board from level one to level three. Each point of karma you have allows you to flip one die to its opposite face once per turn. Opposite faces are: one and six, two and five, three and four. This is devastatingly powerful because it lets you turn a useless die into exactly the value you need. Need a six for a palace chamber? Flip your one. Need a one for the river? Flip your six. Gaining karma should be a priority early in the game. You gain karma from certain river spaces, palace actions, and track bonus spaces.

walkthrough.title: Gaining Extra Workers
walkthrough.text: You start the game with only three workers, which limits how many actions you can take each round. However, you can gain up to two additional workers during the game for a maximum of five. You earn an extra worker when your fame marker reaches position fifteen on the fame track, and another when your money marker reaches position twenty on the money track. There is also a river space that grants a worker when your boat crosses the bridge. More workers means more actions per round, which accelerates your engine significantly. Reaching these thresholds early is a major strategic advantage.

walkthrough.title: Bonus Spaces on the Tracks
walkthrough.text: As you advance along the fame and money tracks, you will land on or pass bonus spaces. These spaces grant various rewards: extra dice, karma upgrades, river movement, building upgrades, and more. Pay attention to upcoming bonus spaces when deciding which track to push — sometimes advancing your money marker two extra spaces nets you a free die that enables your next big move. These bonuses compound over time, so early track advancement creates a snowball effect.

walkthrough.title: End of Round — Retrieve Workers
walkthrough.text: When all players have placed all their workers or passed, the round ends. Every player retrieves all their workers from the board and places them back in their personal supply. The board resets — all action spaces are open again. There is no round limit and no round scoring. The game simply continues round after round until someone triggers the end condition. The first player marker may rotate depending on your group's preference, though the base rules keep the same starting player.

walkthrough.title: Winning the Game
walkthrough.text: The game ends immediately when a player's fame marker and money marker meet or pass each other on the track. That player wins. This can happen at any point during any player's turn — even in the middle of resolving an action. If two players' markers would meet or pass on the same turn due to a chain of effects, the active player wins. If multiple players' markers meet at the end of a round due to bonus spaces, the player whose turn triggered the end wins. There is no final round — the moment the tracks converge, the game is over.

summary.title: The Goal — Two Tracks, One Race

First player whose markers meet or pass each other wins immediately
Every action should push one or both markers closer together

Most spaces hold only 1 worker — blocked once occupied

No fixed round limit — game ends when tracks meet

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Mogul India
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through the first few rounds of a three-player game. We will follow Player 1, Player 2, and Player 3 as they take different approaches. Player 1 will focus on building their province, Player 2 will pursue the river, and Player 3 will make some common mistakes so you can learn from them. Follow along even if your player count is different.

walkthrough.title: Starting Resources
walkthrough.text: Each player has their Kali statue loaded with starting dice — Player 1 has one die, Player 2 has two, and Player 3 has three. Everyone has three workers, starts at karma level one, and has their fame and money markers at the starting positions. The province boards are empty except for the central residence. The board is wide open with all action spaces available. Let's begin.

walkthrough.title: Player 1: Visit the Palace Terrace
walkthrough.text: Player 1 only has one die right now, so building is not yet practical. Instead, Player 1 places a worker on the Palace terrace — a free action space that grants one die of a specific color. Player 1 takes a yellow die, rolls it immediately, and places it on their Kali statue. Now Player 1 has two dice and more options for their next turn. Getting dice early is essential because dice fuel everything.

walkthrough.title: Why That Was Smart
walkthrough.text: Player 1 recognized that with only one die, they could not do much building or river sailing. Rather than waste a worker on a weak action, they grabbed a free die to build up resources first. In Rajas of the Ganges, your first few turns are almost always about accumulating dice. You need a critical mass of dice before the big scoring actions become worthwhile.

walkthrough.title: Player 2: Sail the River
walkthrough.text: Player 2 has two dice and decides to try the Harbor. They place a worker on a Harbor space and spend a die showing two. Their boat advances two spaces along the river. The first space grants a free green die — Player 2 rolls it and places it on their Kali. The second space grants one karma point, so Player 2 advances their karma track from level one to level two. Two bonuses from one action — the river is incredibly efficient.

walkthrough.title: River Efficiency
walkthrough.text: Notice what just happened. Player 2 spent one die and one worker, but got a new die plus a karma upgrade. The die is replaced, and karma is permanent. The river is one of the best investments in the early game because it gives you resources that fuel future turns. A low die value like one, two, or three is perfect for the river — do not waste high-value dice here when low ones work just as well.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Mistake: Rushing to Build
walkthrough.text: Player 3 has three starting dice and is eager to build. They place a worker on the Quarry, pay the money cost, and spend two dice to buy a province tile with a building. They place the tile on their province board, connecting the road to their residence. The building gives them a small fame boost. But now Player 3 has only one die left on their Kali, and their remaining two workers have very limited options.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Don't Spend All Your Dice Early
walkthrough.text: Player 3's mistake was classic — they went for an exciting building action before securing enough dice to sustain their engine. Now they are stuck with one die and limited options. Compare that to Player 2, who spent one die on the river and immediately got one back plus karma. The lesson: in the early game, prioritize actions that give you more dice or karma before spending dice on buildings. Build your resource base first, then build your province.

walkthrough.title: Player 1: Use the Balcony
walkthrough.text: Player 1 now has two dice but wants more flexibility. They place a worker on a Palace balcony and trade one die for two dice of a different color. Now Player 1 has three dice. The balcony is a great way to both increase your dice count and shift to the colors you need. Player 1 is methodically building up a strong dice supply before committing to big actions.

walkthrough.title: Player 2: Hit a Palace Chamber
walkthrough.text: Player 2 has karma level two now, which means they can flip two dice per turn. They have a die showing one, and they need a six for a powerful palace chamber. They spend one karma to flip the one to a six. Then they place a worker on chamber six — the Portuguese — spending the six-value die for a strong reward of resources and track advancement. This is karma in action: turning the worst possible roll into the best possible roll.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Mistake: Ignoring Karma
walkthrough.text: Player 3 has a die showing four and wants to use palace chamber three, which requires a three. They are still at karma level one, so they could flip the four to a three — wait, that is not how opposite faces work. Four flips to three, so actually they could do it! But Player 3 does not realize this and skips the chamber, choosing the free outer terrace for two money and a reroll instead. Player 3 forgot that opposite faces are one-six, two-five, and three-four. A four flips to a three perfectly.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Learn the Flip Pairs
walkthrough.text: Memorize the three flip pairs right now: one and six, two and five, three and four. They always add up to seven. When you look at any die on your Kali, you should instantly see its flip value. A seemingly useless one becomes a powerful six. A mediocre three becomes a useful four. Karma transforms your luck into precision. Always check if flipping a die unlocks a better action before settling for something weaker.

walkthrough.title: End of Round 1
walkthrough.text: All players have placed their three workers. Time to retrieve them all. Look at the board — Player 1 has four dice and a growing supply. Player 2 has advanced on the river with strong karma. Player 3 has a building on their province but is running low on dice. Everyone picks up their workers and all spaces reopen. Round two begins.

walkthrough.title: Round 2: Player 1 Builds Smart
walkthrough.text: Now Player 1 has four dice — enough to buy a solid tile. They visit the Quarry, pay the money, and spend two colored dice to grab a tile with both a building and a market stall. They place it connecting to their residence, earning fame for the building and setting up a market stall for future money scoring. Because Player 1 waited until they had plenty of dice, they still have two dice remaining for their other two workers this round.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Recovery
walkthrough.text: Player 3 learned from round one. This time they start by visiting the Palace terrace to gain a free die, then use the balcony to trade one die for two. By the end of round two, Player 3 has rebuilt their dice supply and is ready to make meaningful moves in round three. Sometimes the best strategy after a mistake is to take a step back, rebuild resources, and then surge forward. Player 3 is back in the game.

walkthrough.title: Watching the Tracks
walkthrough.text: After two rounds, check the track positions. Player 2 has the most fame from river bonuses. Player 1 has balanced advancement on both tracks thanks to their building-and-market tile. Player 3 is behind but catching up. Remember — it does not matter how far you are on either track individually. What matters is how close your two markers are to meeting. A player at fame twelve and money ten is closer to winning than a player at fame twenty and money five.

walkthrough.title: The Market Scoring Move
walkthrough.text: Let's see a market scoring in action. Player 1 places a worker on the Market area and chooses assorted goods. They have three different types of market stalls on their province board, so they score three money, advancing their money marker three spaces. As they advance, they land on a bonus space that grants a free die. This is the snowball effect — scoring money triggered a bonus that gives them more resources to score more in the future.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have seen all four action areas in play, watched how dice management drives the entire game, and learned from Player 3's mistakes. You understand karma flipping, river efficiency, and why patience with dice pays off. The key concepts to carry forward: accumulate dice before spending them, invest in karma early, use the river for resource efficiency, and always think about which track needs the push to close the gap.

walkthrough.title: Before You Rule
walkthrough.text:
Before you start your full game, here are three things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for strategy on balancing your two tracks and managing your dice economy.

Second — reference the Appendix for a quick guide to all palace chambers, river bonuses, and key terms.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun ruling the Ganges!

summary.title: Before You Rule
bullets: Check out General Tips for track-balancing and dice economy strategy
Reference the Appendix for palace chambers, river bonuses, and key terms
Ask your personal GM Guide — tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we get into strategy, here are the mistakes that trip up almost every new player. First, spending all your dice early without building a pipeline to get more — this leaves you taking weak free actions while opponents snowball. Second, pushing only one track and ignoring the other — remember, both markers must meet, so a lopsided strategy means a longer path to victory. Third, ignoring karma — without karma, you are at the mercy of dice rolls, while opponents with karma two or three can hit any value they want. Fourth, hoarding dice on your Kali without spending them — ten dice sitting idle means you are missing the actions that convert dice into track advancement. Fifth, neglecting the market stalls — markets are the most efficient way to push the money track, and players who skip them fall behind on money.

walkthrough.title: Balance Both Tracks
walkthrough.text: The single most important strategic concept in Rajas of the Ganges is track balance. Your fame and money markers need to meet, so pushing one track to fifty while the other sits at five means you still have a long way to go. The optimal strategy advances both tracks roughly in parallel. After each action, ask yourself: which marker is further from meeting the other? Push that one next. Some actions advance both tracks simultaneously, like certain palace chambers and river bonuses. These are extremely valuable because they close the gap from both sides.

walkthrough.title: The Dice Economy
walkthrough.text: Think of dice as your fuel supply. Every major action consumes dice, and if you run dry, you are stuck taking free actions on the Palace terrace or outer terrace. Build a sustainable dice economy by mixing dice-gaining actions with dice-spending actions. The river, palace terraces, and balconies all generate dice. Ideally you never want to drop below two or three dice on your Kali, so you always have options on your next turn. When you are flush with dice, that is the time to make big building or chamber plays.

walkthrough.title: Invest in Karma Early
walkthrough.text: Karma is the great equalizer in a dice game. At karma level one, you can flip one die per turn. At level two, you can flip two. At level three, you can flip three — meaning nearly any die can become any value you need. The earlier you invest in karma, the more turns you benefit from it. Getting to karma two by the end of round one or early round two is a strong goal. River spaces and certain palace actions grant karma. Do not wait until the late game to get karma — by then, the accumulated benefit is lost.

walkthrough.title: Province Planning
walkthrough.text: When placing tiles on your province board, think several tiles ahead. Roads must connect, and a tile placed in the wrong spot can block a much better placement later. Prioritize tiles that give you both a building type you need and road connections in useful directions. Market stalls are deceptively powerful — each different type you collect makes your assorted goods market scoring more valuable. Try to collect at least three or four different market types for a strong mid-game money push. And remember that building upgrades are powerful — each of the four building types can be upgraded once for a significant bonus.

walkthrough.title: Worker Threshold Priority
walkthrough.text: Gaining your fourth and fifth workers should be a high priority. You start with three workers, which means three actions per round. Gaining a fourth worker gives you thirty-three percent more actions every single round for the rest of the game. That extra worker compounds massively over time. Push your fame to fifteen and money to twenty as early as possible to unlock those extra workers. Also watch for the river bridge that grants a worker. Players who reach four or five workers first have a huge tempo advantage over those stuck at three.

walkthrough.title: Reading Your Opponents
walkthrough.text: Since most action spaces can only hold one worker, watching your opponents is critical. If you see someone eyeing the same Quarry tile you want, consider taking it first. If a powerful palace chamber is about to be blocked, prioritize it. In a two-player game, blocking is less intense but still matters. In a four-player game, the board gets crowded fast and you may need to take your most important action first each round rather than setting up. Pay attention to how many workers each opponent has remaining — if someone has already placed all their workers, you have free reign to take any open space.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't spend all dice early — keep a pipeline of dice-generating actions
Don't push only one track — both markers must meet
Don't ignore karma — it transforms luck into precision
Don't hoard dice idle OR run dry — maintain 2-3 dice minimum
Don't neglect markets — they are the best money-track engine

summary.title: Balance Both Tracks
bullets: Advance both fame and money roughly in parallel
After each action: which marker is further from meeting? Push that one
Actions that advance both tracks simultaneously are extremely valuable

summary.title: The Dice Economy
bullets: Mix dice-gaining actions (river, terraces, balconies) with dice-spending actions
Never drop below 2-3 dice on your Kali
When flush with dice, make big building or chamber plays

summary.title: Invest in Karma Early
bullets: Get to karma 2 by end of round 1 or early round 2
Each karma level = 1 die flip per turn
Earlier investment = more turns of accumulated benefit

summary.title: Province Planning
bullets: Plan tile placement several tiles ahead — roads must connect
Collect 3-4 different market types for strong assorted goods scoring
Upgrade buildings when possible — each of 4 types upgrades once for a bonus

summary.title: Worker Threshold Priority
bullets: 4th worker = 33% more actions per round — massive compound value
Push fame to 15 and money to 20 for extra workers
River bridge also grants a worker — plan to cross it
Players with 4-5 workers have huge tempo advantage

summary.title: Reading Your Opponents
bullets: Most spaces hold 1 worker — watch for contested actions
Take your most critical action first in high player-count games
When opponents are out of workers, you have free reign
Block opponents from tiles or chambers that would let them surge ahead

walkthrough.title: The Fame Rush Strategy
walkthrough.text: One proven approach is to rush the fame track aggressively while keeping money at a minimum viable level. Focus on buildings over markets in the Quarry. Use palace chambers that grant fame — the Dancer and Great Mogul are your targets. Push fame fast enough to unlock the fourth worker at position fifteen, then use the extra worker advantage to grind out money with market scorings and trade actions. The risk is falling behind on money, so you need to eventually pivot. The advantage is an early worker unlock and access to fame-track bonus spaces that feed your engine.

walkthrough.title: The Money Engine Strategy
walkthrough.text: The opposite approach: prioritize market stalls on your province board and score them repeatedly. Build a diverse province with four or five different market types so assorted goods scoring gives you five or more money per activation. Combine this with the goods-of-one-kind scoring using high-value dice for massive single-turn money jumps. The money track has the fourth-worker unlock at position twenty — further than fame's fifteen — but the money track also tends to have strong bonus spaces. This strategy is slower to start but can accelerate rapidly in the mid-game once your market diversity is established.

walkthrough.title: The River Specialist
walkthrough.text: The river is arguably the most efficient area in the game because every space grants a bonus. The river specialist strategy focuses on sailing as far as possible, using low-value dice to advance the boat and collecting a cascade of rewards: free dice, karma upgrades, money, fame, market scorings, and even extra workers at the bridge. The key insight is that river bonuses often feed themselves — gaining a die from a river space lets you sail further on a future turn. Combine river advancement with karma investment so you can always produce low-value dice for sailing.

walkthrough.title: Karma Maximization
walkthrough.text: Getting to karma level three as fast as possible is a legitimate win condition accelerator. At karma three, you can flip three dice per turn, meaning you can hit virtually any combination of colors and values you need. This unlocks every palace chamber regardless of what you roll. It lets you use exactly the right value for goods-of-one-kind market scoring. It lets you consistently produce ones, twos, and threes for the river. The investment in karma pays off on every single turn for the rest of the game. Prioritize karma from river spaces, the Yogi chamber (three), and track bonus spaces.

walkthrough.title: Tile Synergy and Upgrades
walkthrough.text: Advanced players think about province tiles as a system, not individual purchases. Each of the four building types can be upgraded once, and the upgrade bonuses are often game-changing. Look for tiles that create long connected roads from your residence to the province edge, as these often trigger road bonuses. Diversify market types early for assorted goods scoring, but also consider concentrating one type for goods-of-one-kind scoring with high-value dice. The best province boards have both diversity and concentration — three or four different types but also three or four copies of one type.

walkthrough.title: Worker Timing and Blocking
walkthrough.text: In experienced groups, the order in which you place workers matters enormously. Your first worker each round should claim the most contested space or the space you absolutely cannot afford to lose. If two players are competing for the same Quarry tile, whoever places first gets it. Holding your last worker for a late-round action lets you see what spaces remain open and pick the best available option. When you have more workers than opponents — say five to their three — you can afford to take less critical actions early, knowing you will get extra turns after they pass.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Acceleration
walkthrough.text: The final push to close the gap between your markers requires a shift in thinking. Stop investing in long-term engine pieces and focus exclusively on actions that advance your tracks. Market scorings become critical — a single assorted goods action can advance money by five or more spaces. Palace chambers that grant fame or dual-track advancement are priorities. The river's late spaces often have powerful bonuses. Watch your opponents' track positions carefully. If someone is within striking distance, you may need to sprint rather than optimize. The player who recognizes the endgame trigger point first has a massive advantage.

summary.title: The Fame Rush Strategy
bullets: Push fame aggressively — buildings over markets, fame-granting chambers
Unlock 4th worker at fame 15 for early tempo advantage
Use extra worker to grind out money in the mid-game
Risk: falling behind on money; requires eventual pivot

summary.title: The Money Engine Strategy
bullets: Build 4-5 different market types for 5+ money per assorted goods scoring
Combine with goods-of-one-kind using high-value dice for big jumps
Slower start but accelerates rapidly once market diversity established
4th worker at money 20 — further than fame 15 but strong bonus spaces

summary.title: The River Specialist
bullets: River is the most efficient area — every space grants a bonus
River bonuses feed themselves: gaining dice enables more sailing
Combine with karma so you can always produce low dice for sailing
Bridge grants extra worker — plan your route to cross it

summary.title: Karma Maximization
bullets: Karma 3 = flip 3 dice per turn = hit any value you need
Unlocks every palace chamber regardless of rolls
Optimizes market scoring, river sailing, and building purchases
Prioritize: river spaces, Yogi chamber (3), track bonus spaces

summary.title: Tile Synergy and Upgrades
bullets: All 4 building types can upgrade once — bonuses are game-changing
Long connected roads to province edge trigger road bonuses
Best provinces: 3-4 different market types AND 3-4 copies of one type
Diversity for assorted goods + concentration for goods-of-one-kind

summary.title: Worker Timing and Blocking
bullets: First worker each round: claim most contested or critical space
Hold last worker to see what remains and pick the best option
With more workers than opponents, you get extra turns after they pass
In 3-4 player games, first-worker priority is critical

summary.title: Endgame Acceleration
bullets: Stop investing in engine — focus on pure track advancement
Market scorings can advance money 5+ spaces in one action
Watch opponents' track positions — sprint if someone is close
Player who recognizes the endgame trigger point first has a huge advantage

entries.definition: The scoring track that advances clockwise around the board. Gained from buildings, palace chambers, river bonuses, and certain actions. The fame marker must meet the money marker to win.

The scoring track that advances counter-clockwise around the board. Gained from market scorings, palace chambers, river bonuses, and certain actions. The money marker must meet the fame marker to win.
Special spaces on the fame and money tracks that grant rewards when you land on or pass them. Bonuses include extra dice, karma, river movement, building upgrades, and more. These compound over time.
When your fame marker reaches or passes position 15, you immediately gain one additional worker. This gives you four workers total, increasing your actions per round by 33 percent.
When your money marker reaches or passes position 20, you immediately gain one additional worker. Combined with the fame threshold and river bridge, you can reach a maximum of five workers.

Each player's personal dice holder. The Kali statue has arms that can hold up to 10 dice at a time. If you would gain an 11th die, you must decline it. Dice are placed here after rolling and removed when spent.

The primary resource in Rajas of the Ganges. Four colors: yellow, orange, green, and brown. When you gain a die, always roll it immediately. Dice are spent by returning them to the supply. Both color and value matter for different actions.

Pieces placed on the main board to take actions. You start with 3 and can gain up to 2 more (max 5). When all players have placed or passed, the round ends and workers are retrieved. Most action spaces hold only one worker.

Each player's personal board where province tiles are placed. The central residence has roads extending outward. All tiles must connect via roads to the residence or existing tiles. Contains buildings and market stalls.

Square tiles purchased from the Quarry. Show buildings (earning fame), market stalls (scored for money), or both. Must be placed connecting roads to your province network. Cannot be moved once placed.

Tracked on your player board from level 1 to 3. Each karma point lets you flip one die to its opposite face per turn. Opposite faces: 1/6, 2/5, 3/4 (always add to 7). Gained from river spaces, palace actions, and track bonuses.

When flipping a die with karma, opposite faces are: 1 becomes 6, 2 becomes 5, 3 becomes 4, and vice versa. Each pair sums to 7. At karma 3, you can flip 3 dice per turn, giving near-total control over die values.

Worker placement area for buying province tiles. Pay a money cost and spend colored dice matching the tile's requirements. The purchased tile must be immediately placed on your province board connecting to existing roads.

Worker placement area for scoring money from market stalls on your province. Two scoring types: assorted goods (one per different type) and goods of one kind (spend a die, count up to die value of one type).

Market scoring method. Score 1 money for each different type of market stall in your province. With 5 different types, you score 5 money. Does not require spending a die. Best when you have diverse market stalls.

Market scoring method. Spend a die and score money equal to the number of market stalls of one type you have, up to the die's value. A die showing 5 with 4 stalls of one type scores 4 money. Best when you concentrate one market type.

Worker placement area with multiple chambers offering varied effects. Includes the outer terrace (free: 2 money + reroll), terrace (free: gain 1 colored die), balconies (trade 1 die for 2), and inner chambers 1-6 (spend die of exact value).

Free Palace action space. Place a worker to gain 2 money and reroll one of your dice. No die cost required. A solid fallback when you are low on dice or need to adjust a die value without spending karma.
Free Palace action space. Place a worker to gain one die of a specific color (determined by the space). Roll the die immediately and place on your Kali. Great for accumulating dice early in the game.
Palace action space. Trade one die from your Kali for two dice of another specified color. Roll both new dice immediately. Increases your die count by one and shifts colors to what you need.

Palace inner chamber. Spend a die showing exactly 1 to gain a substantial fame or money bonus. One of the most rewarding chambers when you can access it. Karma makes producing a 1 trivial by flipping a 6.

Palace inner chamber. Spend a die showing exactly 2. The Dancer advances you on both the fame and money tracks simultaneously. Extremely valuable because dual-track advancement closes the gap from both sides.
Palace inner chamber. Spend a die showing exactly 3. The Yogi grants karma points and possibly additional dice. A priority target for players pursuing the karma maximization strategy.
Palace inner chamber. Spend a die showing exactly 4. The Raja grants province-building advantages, which can include discounts on tiles or free tile placement benefits.
Palace inner chamber. Spend a die showing exactly 5. The Master Builder grants building upgrades for your province, which provide ongoing bonuses for each upgraded building type.
Palace inner chamber. Spend a die showing exactly 6. The Portuguese provides trading opportunities and valuable resources. Karma makes producing a 6 trivial by flipping a 1.

Worker placement area for advancing your boat along the river. Spend a die showing 1, 2, or 3 to advance your boat that many spaces. Each river space grants bonuses: dice, karma, money, fame, upgrades, or market scorings.
Each player's boat on the river track. Starts at the dock and advances downstream as you spend dice at Harbor action spaces. The boat never moves backward. Landing on each space triggers its bonus.

A special river space that grants an extra worker when your boat crosses it. This is the third way to gain workers (along with fame 15 and money 20). Planning your river route to cross the bridge is a strong strategy.

Province tiles can contain one of four building types. Each type can be upgraded once during the game for a permanent bonus. Buildings primarily contribute to fame advancement. Upgraded buildings provide additional rewards when triggered.

Each of the four building types can be upgraded once, gained from certain palace chambers, river spaces, or track bonuses. Upgrades provide powerful ongoing effects. Upgrading all four types is a strong long-term goal.

Icons on province tiles representing goods for sale. Come in multiple types. Scored at the Market action area via assorted goods (diversity) or goods of one kind (concentration). More stalls and more types mean more money per scoring.

Province tiles have roads on their edges. When placing a tile, its roads must connect to your existing road network from the central residence. Connected roads extending to the province edge may trigger road bonuses.

When all players have placed all workers or passed, the round ends. All workers are retrieved from the board and all action spaces reopen. There is no round limit — the game continues until a player's tracks meet.

If you have no workers left to place, you must pass. You cannot voluntarily pass if you have workers remaining. Once all players have passed, the round ends. Having more workers than opponents gives you extra late-round turns.

The game ends immediately when any player's fame and money markers meet or pass each other on the board. That player wins. If tied (multiple players' markers meet simultaneously), the active player who triggered the end wins.
